prompt: You are given a list of closed integer intervals `intervals`, each as a 2-element list `[l, r]` with l <= r (values may be negative; intervals may be given in any order and may overlap). Two intervals are merged into the same group if their integer coverage overlaps OR merely TOUCHES with at most a one-unit gap, i.e. intervals [1,3] and [4,6] merge (gap of exactly 1 between 3 and 4 counts as touching), but [1,3] and [5,6] do NOT (gap of 2). Merging is transitive. After computing the final set of maximal merged intervals, return (as an int) the number of merged intervals whose integer-coverage count (r - l + 1) is ODD. Signature: `entry(intervals)`. If `intervals` is empty, return 0.

Starting from the integer 1, you may repeatedly apply any of these three operations: x -> x+3, x -> x*2, x -> x-1. At all times the current value must stay within the inclusive range [1, 2*n+5]. Return (as an int) the minimum number of operations needed to reach exactly the integer `n` (n >= 1). It is always reachable. Signature: `entry(n)`. For n == 1 the answer is 0.

You are given a list of integers `arr` (values may repeat). In one move you may take any single element out of the array and reinsert it at the very FRONT or the very BACK of the array. Return (as an int) the minimum number of moves required to make the array non-decreasing. Signature: `entry(arr)`. For length 0 or 1 the answer is 0. (Hint: the elements you never move must, after the moves collapse them together, already be non-decreasing and occupy a contiguous slice of the fully-sorted multiset.)

Two players alternate turns on a single pile of `n` stones (n >= 0). On your turn you must either remove exactly 1 stone, OR — only if the pile currently holds an EVEN number of stones — remove exactly half of them (k -> k/2 removed, leaving k/2). The player who removes the last stone wins. The first player moves first. With n == 0 there are no stones and the player to move cannot move and therefore loses. Return (as a bool) True if the FIRST player wins under optimal play, otherwise False. Signature: `entry(n)`.

You must cover every tag in the set {0, 1, ..., T-1}. You are given `items`, a list where each item is `[mask, cost]`: `mask` is an integer bitmask whose set bit i means the item covers tag i, and `cost` is a positive integer. Choose a subset of items (each item usable at most once) so that the bitwise OR of chosen masks has all T low bits set. Return (as an int) the minimum total cost of such a subset, or -1 if it is impossible. Signature: `entry(items, T)`. If T == 0 the requirement is empty and the answer is 0.

Given a lowercase string `s` and an integer `k`, count (as an int) how many of the length-`k` contiguous substrings of `s` can have their characters rearranged to form a palindrome. A multiset of characters can form a palindrome iff at most one character has an odd count (when k is odd) or zero characters have an odd count (when k is even). Return the count of qualifying windows. Signature: `entry(s, k)`. If k <= 0 or k > len(s), return 0.

You are given a binary grid `grid` (a list of equal-length rows of 0/1; up to 3x3). A 'press' on a cell toggles that cell AND its orthogonal (up/down/left/right) in-bounds neighbors. You may press any cell any number of times in any order. Return (as a bool) True if it is possible to reach the all-zero grid from the given grid, otherwise False. Signature: `entry(grid)`. An empty grid (no rows) returns True.

Given a list `a` of integers (possibly negative, possibly with duplicates, length up to 10), consider every subset of `a` (including the empty subset, whose sum is 0). Return (as an int) the number of DISTINCT subset sums. Signature: `entry(a)`. For the empty list the answer is 1 (only the empty subset, sum 0).

You are given an integer `n` (number of nodes labeled 0..n-1) and `edges`, a list of `[u, v]` pairs forming a TREE (n-1 edges, connected, no cycles; for n==1 edges is empty). Return (as an int) the number of UNORDERED pairs {u, v} whose unique tree-path has length EXACTLY 2 edges. Signature: `entry(n, edges)`.

Given a digit string `s` (characters '0'-'9') and an integer `m` (m >= 1), count (as an int) the number of NON-EMPTY contiguous substrings of `s` that, interpreted as a base-10 integer, are divisible by `m`. A substring with a leading zero is NOT counted UNLESS it is exactly the single character "0". So "0" counts (value 0, divisible by any m), but "01", "007" etc. are never counted. Different positions producing the same value are counted separately. Signature: `entry(s, m)`. Empty string returns 0.

You must schedule talks into rooms. `talks` is a list of `[start, end)` half-open integer intervals (start < end). After a talk ends at time `e` in a room, that room needs a cleanup of `g` time units (g >= 0) before another talk may START in it; a new talk may start at exactly time e+g (the boundary is allowed). A room hosts at most one talk at a time. Return (as an int) the minimum number of rooms needed so that every talk can be hosted. Signature: `entry(talks, g)`. Empty `talks` returns 0.

You are given a grid `grid` (a list of equal-length rows of non-negative integers; up to 3x3) and an integer `k`. A path goes from the top-left cell (0,0) to the bottom-right cell (R-1,C-1) moving only RIGHT or DOWN one step at a time. The XOR-value of a path is the bitwise XOR of all cells it visits (including both endpoints). Return (as an int) the number of distinct paths whose XOR-value equals exactly `k`. Two paths are distinct if their sequences of moves differ. Signature: `entry(grid, k)`. An empty grid (no rows) returns 0.

Given two strings `s` and `t`, consider every cyclic left-rotation of `s` by r positions for r = 0, 1, ..., len(s)-1 (rotation r is s[r:] + s[:r]). Return (as an int) the number of distinct rotation indices r for which that rotation of `s` is an ANAGRAM of `t` (same multiset of characters). Signature: `entry(s, t)`. If len(s) != len(t) return 0. If both strings are empty, there is exactly one rotation (the empty string), which is an anagram of the empty `t`, so return 1.

Count (as an int) the number of balanced bracket sequences of length 2*`n` (made of '(' and ')') such that at no prefix does the number of currently-open brackets exceed `D`. Formally, scanning left to right, '(' adds 1 and ')' subtracts 1 from a running balance; the balance must always stay in the range [0, D] and end at 0. Return the count. Signature: `entry(n, D)`. For n == 0 there is exactly one sequence (the empty one), so return 1. If D == 0 and n > 0, return 0.

Given a list `a` of non-negative integers, define the prefix-gcd array g where g[i] = gcd(a[0], a[1], ..., a[i]). Return (as an int) the number of DISTINCT values appearing in g. Use the convention gcd of a running value with the next element via standard math.gcd (gcd(0,x)=x). Signature: `entry(a)`. For the empty list return 0.

Given a list `a` of integers (a multiset, length up to 8), consider every possible ordering (permutation) of all its elements placed in a line. For an ordering, its cost is the MAXIMUM absolute difference between adjacent elements. Return (as an int) the minimum achievable cost over all orderings. Signature: `entry(a)`. For length 0 or 1 the cost is 0 (no adjacent pairs).

You are given a list of integers `nums` and an integer `K`.

Select a subsequence (any subset of elements, picked in their original left-to-right order) such that any two CONSECUTIVE picked elements have DIFFERENT parity (one odd, one even). You may pick AT MOST `K` elements. Maximize the sum of the picked elements.

Implement `entry(nums, K)` returning the maximum achievable sum as an int. If picking nothing is best (e.g. K==0 or nums is empty, or all sums would be considered), the answer is 0 (the empty selection has sum 0). All values are non-negative integers.

Return a single int.

You are given a grid of non-negative integers `grid` (a list of rows, each row a list of ints, all rows equal length). A token walks from the top-left cell (0,0) to the bottom-right cell, each step moving either one cell RIGHT or one cell DOWN.

Along a path, the token COLLECTS a cell's value if and only if that value is STRICTLY GREATER than every value collected earlier on the path. The starting cell (0,0) is always collected. Equivalently, the collected values are the strict running maxima (new records) of the sequence of cell values along the path.

Implement `entry(grid)` returning the maximum possible total of collected values over all monotone right/down paths, as an int. If the grid is empty, return 0.

Return a single int.

Two players play a game on a collection of stone piles given as a list `piles` of pile sizes (non-negative ints). Players alternate turns; the first player moves first.

On your turn you must pick one pile whose size is at least 2 and remove exactly floor(size/2) stones from it (so a pile of size s becomes s - floor(s/2) = ceil(s/2)). A pile of size 0 or 1 cannot be chosen (removing floor would be 0 stones, which is not a legal move). A player who has no legal move loses.

Implement `entry(piles)` returning True if the first player has a winning strategy under optimal play, otherwise False.

Return a bool.

You are given a list of non-negative integers `nums`. Count the number of unordered pairs of indices (i, j) with i < j such that BOTH conditions hold:
1. nums[i] AND nums[j] == 0 (their set bits are disjoint), and
2. the number of set bits in (nums[i] OR nums[j]) is EVEN.

Implement `entry(nums)` returning that count as an int. (Note: 0 has an even bit count of 0.)

Return a single int.

You are given a list of closed integer intervals `intervals`, each as a 2-element list [l, r] with l <= r. You may keep any subset of the intervals. Define the load at an integer point t as the number of kept intervals that cover t (i.e. l <= t <= r). The constraint is that at NO integer point may the load exceed 2.

Implement `entry(intervals)` returning the maximum number of intervals you can keep while never exceeding load 2 at any integer point. If `intervals` is empty, return 0.

Return a single int.

You are given a list of non-negative integers `a`. For each cut position i with 1 <= i <= len(a)-1, split into a prefix a[0..i-1] and a suffix a[i..len(a)-1]. Count the number of cut positions i such that BOTH:
1. XOR of the prefix == XOR of the suffix, and
2. sum of the prefix is STRICTLY GREATER than sum of the suffix.

Implement `entry(a)` returning that count as an int. If len(a) < 2, return 0.

Return a single int.
reference:
def entry(a):
    n = len(a)
    if n < 2:
        return 0
    total = sum(a)
    totalx = 0
    for v in a:
        totalx ^= v
    pref = 0
    prefx = 0
    cnt = 0
    for i in range(n - 1):
        pref += a[i]
        prefx ^= a[i]
        sufx = totalx ^ prefx
        sufsum = total - pref
        if prefx == sufx and pref > sufsum:
            cnt += 1
    return cnt

prompt:
You are given a list of integers `a`. For each index i, let f(i) be the smallest index j > i such that a[j] is STRICTLY GREATER than a[i], or -1 if no such index exists.

Count the number of indices i for which f(i) != -1 AND (f(i) - i) is ODD.

Implement `entry(a)` returning that count as an int.

Return a single int.

Count the number of ways to completely tile a 1 x n strip using tiles of size 1 (monomino) and size 2 (domino), with the extra rule that NO TWO size-2 tiles may be placed immediately next to each other (two dominoes cannot be adjacent in the tiling order). Tiles are placed left to right with no gaps or overlaps.

Implement `entry(n)` returning the number of valid tilings modulo 1000000007. For n == 0 (empty strip) there is exactly 1 way (the empty tiling).

Return a single int.

You are given a list of integers `a`. Find the length of the LONGEST contiguous subarray a[i..j] (i <= j) satisfying:

  (max of the subarray) - (min of the subarray) <= max(a[i], a[j])

that is, the spread of the subarray is at most the larger of its two endpoint values.

Implement `entry(a)` returning that maximum length as an int. If `a` is empty, return 0. A single element always satisfies the condition (spread 0 <= the element, assuming non-negative values are NOT guaranteed — but a single element has max==min==both endpoints so the condition is 0 <= a[i] which may fail for negatives; nonetheless apply the rule literally). All test values are non-negative.

Return a single int.

Count the binary strings of length n (characters '0' and '1') that contain NEITHER the substring "010" NOR the substring "101". Equivalently, no three consecutive characters form an alternating pattern.

Implement `entry(n)` returning that count modulo 1000000007. For n == 0 there is exactly 1 string (the empty string).

Return a single int.

You are given `n` nodes labeled 0..n-1 and a list `edges`, where each edge is a 3-element list [u, v, t]. If t == 0 the two endpoints must receive the SAME color; if t == 1 they must receive DIFFERENT colors. Colors are from {0, 1}.

Implement `entry(n, edges)` returning the number of valid colorings of all n nodes (assignments of a color to every node) modulo 1000000007. If no valid coloring exists, return 0.

Return a single int.

You are given a list of positive integers `nums`. Count the number of NON-EMPTY subsets (chosen by index, so equal values at different indices are distinct) whose product is a perfect square.

Implement `entry(nums)` returning that count modulo 1000000007. If `nums` is empty, return 0.

Return a single int.

You are given a tree with `n` nodes labeled 0..n-1, described by `edges` (a list of [u, v] pairs, exactly n-1 edges forming a tree when n >= 1), and a list `w` of node weights (non-negative ints). Choose a subset S of nodes that is an INDEPENDENT SET (no two chosen nodes are adjacent) AND that MUST include the root node 0. Maximize the total weight of S.

Implement `entry(n, edges, w)` returning that maximum total weight as an int. If n == 0, return 0. (Choosing just {0} is always valid, so the answer is at least w[0] when n >= 1.)

Return a single int.
reference:
def entry(n, edges, w):
    if n == 0:
        return 0
    import sys
    from collections import defaultdict
    sys.setrecursionlimit(10000)
    g = defaultdict(list)
    for u, v in edges:
        g[u].append(v)
        g[v].append(u)
    def dfs(u, parent):
        take = w[u]
        skip = 0
        for c in g[u]:
            if c == parent:
                continue
            ct, cs = dfs(c, u)
            take += cs
            skip += max(ct, cs)
        return take, skip
    take, skip = dfs(0, -1)
    return take

prompt:
For a positive integer x, index its decimal digits from the RIGHT starting at 0 (the units digit is position 0). Call x "balanced" if the sum of digits at EVEN positions equals the sum of digits at ODD positions.

Given an integer N, count how many integers x in the range [1, N] are balanced.

Implement `entry(N)` returning that count as an int. If N < 1, return 0.

Return a single int.

You are given a string `s` consisting only of the characters 'A' and 'B'. You may delete any characters you choose. Find the MINIMUM number of deletions so that the resulting string does NOT contain "ABBA" as a SUBSEQUENCE (a subsequence need not be contiguous).

Implement `entry(s)` returning the minimum number of deletions as an int. If `s` is empty, return 0.

Return a single int.

You are given a grid `grid` (a list of equal-length strings) made of three cell types: '.' (empty, beam passes straight through), '/' and '\\' (mirrors). A light beam enters the top-left cell (0,0) moving to the RIGHT.

Reflection rules: on '/', a beam moving right turns up, left turns down, up turns right, down turns left. On '\\', a beam moving right turns down, left turns up, up turns left, down turns right. The beam continues until it leaves the grid.

Implement `entry(grid)` returning the number of DISTINCT cells the beam visits before exiting (including the start cell). If the beam never exits (it enters a loop), return -1. If the grid is empty, return 0.

Return a single int.
reference:
def entry(grid):
    R = len(grid)
    if R == 0:
        return 0
    C = len(grid[0])
    dr = [0, 1, 0, -1]
    dc = [1, 0, -1, 0]
    r, c, d = 0, 0, 0
    seen_states = set()
    visited = set()
    while 0 <= r < R and 0 <= c < C:
        state = (r, c, d)
        if state in seen_states:
            return -1
        seen_states.add(state)
        visited.add((r, c))
        ch = grid[r][c]
        if ch == '/':
            d = {0: 3, 3: 0, 2: 1, 1: 2}[d]
        elif ch == '\\':
            d = {0: 1, 1: 0, 2: 3, 3: 2}[d]
        r += dr[d]
        c += dc[d]
    return len(visited)
bruteforce:
def entry(grid):
    R = len(grid)
    if R == 0:
        return 0
    C = len(grid[0])
    dr = [0, 1, 0, -1]
    dc = [1, 0, -1, 0]
    r, c, d = 0, 0, 0
    visited = set()
    limit = 4 * R * C + 5
    steps = 0
    while 0 <= r < R and 0 <= c < C:
        visited.add((r, c))
        ch = grid[r][c]
        if ch == '/':
            d = {0: 3, 3: 0, 2: 1, 1: 2}[d]
        elif ch == '\\':
            d = {0: 1, 1: 0, 2: 3, 3: 2}[d]
        r += dr[d]
        c += dc[d]
        steps += 1
        if steps > limit:
            return -1
    return len(visited)

prompt:
There are n children in a line, with ratings given by the list `r`. Each child must receive at least 1 candy (candies are positive integers). A child is a "strict peak" if they have at least one neighbor and their rating is STRICTLY GREATER than every existing neighbor's rating. Every strict-peak child must receive strictly more candies than each of their neighbors. Children that are not strict peaks have no constraint beyond receiving at least 1 candy.

Implement `entry(r)` returning the minimum total number of candies needed. If `r` is empty, return 0.

Return a single int.

You are given a tree with n nodes labeled 0..n-1 (rooted at node 0), described by an edge list `edges` (each `[a,b]` an undirected edge; `edges` has exactly n-1 entries, or is empty when n==1). You color every node with one of `colors` colors numbered 0,1,...,colors-1, subject to the rule that adjacent nodes must receive different colors. Assigning color c to a node costs exactly c (the color's index). The total cost of a coloring is the sum of these per-node costs.

Implement `entry(n, edges, colors, budget)` returning the number of valid colorings whose total cost equals exactly `budget`, taken modulo 1000000007. Return an int.

You are given a list `intervals`, each element `[l, r, w]` describing a half-open interval [l, r) with integer endpoints (l < r) and a positive integer weight w, plus an integer `cap` (>= 0). Choose a subset of the intervals to maximize the total weight, subject to the constraint that at no point on the line do more than `cap` chosen intervals overlap. Overlap at a point p means l <= p < r. (Equivalently: the maximum number of chosen intervals covering any single point must not exceed `cap`.)

Implement `entry(intervals, cap)` returning the maximum achievable total weight as an int. If `intervals` is empty or `cap` is 0, the answer is 0.
reference:
def entry(intervals, cap):
    n=len(intervals)
    if n==0: return 0
    pts=sorted(set([x for iv in intervals for x in (iv[0],iv[1])]))
    idx={p:i for i,p in enumerate(pts)}
    m=len(pts)
    N=m+2
    S=m; T=m+1
    graph=[[] for _ in range(N)]
    def add_edge(u,v,capa,cost):
        graph[u].append([v,capa,cost,len(graph[v])])
        graph[v].append([u,0,-cost,len(graph[u])-1])
    INF=float('inf')
    for i in range(m-1):
        add_edge(i,i+1,cap,0)
    add_edge(S,0,cap,0)
    add_edge(m-1,T,cap,0)
    for l,r,w in intervals:
        add_edge(idx[l],idx[r],1,-w)
    total_cost=0
    flow=0
    from collections import deque
    while flow<cap:
        dist=[INF]*N
        inq=[False]*N
        prevv=[-1]*N; preve=[-1]*N
        dist[S]=0
        dq=deque([S]); inq[S]=True
        while dq:
            u=dq.popleft(); inq[u]=False
            for ei,e in enumerate(graph[u]):
                v,capa,cost,rev=e
                if capa>0 and dist[u]+cost<dist[v]:
                    dist[v]=dist[u]+cost
                    prevv[v]=u; preve[v]=ei
                    if not inq[v]:
                        inq[v]=True; dq.append(v)
        if dist[T]==INF: break
        if dist[T]>=0:
            break
        d=cap-flow
        v=T
        while v!=S:
            d=min(d,graph[prevv[v]][preve[v]][1])
            v=prevv[v]
        v=T
        while v!=S:
            graph[prevv[v]][preve[v]][1]-=d
            rev=graph[prevv[v]][preve[v]][3]
            graph[v][rev][1]+=d
            v=prevv[v]
        flow+=d
        total_cost+=d*dist[T]
    return -total_cost

prompt:
Count the strings of length n over an alphabet of k symbols (0..k-1) that satisfy BOTH of these conditions: (1) no two adjacent characters are equal (s[i] != s[i+1] for all valid i), and (2) no character equals the one two positions before it (s[i] != s[i+2] for all valid i). Equivalently, every window of three consecutive characters has all three distinct, and every window of two has them distinct.

Implement `entry(n, k)` returning the count modulo 1000000007 as an int. By convention there is exactly 1 string of length 0 (the empty string).

You are given a rectangular `grid` of non-negative integers with R rows and C columns (R,C >= 1). A path starts at cell (0,0) and ends at cell (R-1,C-1), moving only right or down one cell at a time. The cost of a path is the sum of the values of all cells it occupies, INCLUDING the start cell (0,0) and the end cell.

Among all such monotone paths whose total cost is EVEN, return the minimum total cost. Implement `entry(grid)` returning that minimum even cost as an int, or -1 if no monotone path has an even total cost.

You are given n items labeled 0..n-1 (1 <= n <= 16) and a list `pairs` of conflicting pairs, each `[a,b]` meaning items a and b conflict. Partition ALL items into groups so that no group contains a conflicting pair (each group is an independent set in the conflict graph). Implement `entry(n, pairs)` returning the minimum possible number of groups (the chromatic number of the conflict graph) as an int.

You are given an array `a` of positive integers (its length is between 0 and 10, values between 1 and 30). Count the number of NON-EMPTY subsequences of `a` (a subsequence is identified by the set of chosen indices, with the original left-to-right order preserved) such that every pair of CONSECUTIVE chosen elements is coprime (their gcd equals 1). A subsequence consisting of a single element always qualifies.

Implement `entry(a)` returning this count modulo 1000000007 as an int. For an empty array the answer is 0.
reference:
from math import gcd
def entry(a):
    MOD=10**9+7
    n=len(a)
    if n==0: return 0
    dp=[0]*n
    total=0
    for i in range(n):
        dp[i]=1
        for j in range(i):
            if gcd(a[j],a[i])==1:
                dp[i]=(dp[i]+dp[j])%MOD
        total=(total+dp[i])%MOD
    return total%MOD

prompt:
You are given a tree with n nodes labeled 0..n-1, described by an edge list `edges` (n-1 undirected edges, or empty when n==1), and an integer K (K >= 0). Count the number of unordered pairs of distinct nodes {u, v} (u < v) whose distance in the tree, measured as the number of edges on the unique path between them, equals exactly K.

Implement `entry(n, edges, K)` returning that count as an int.

Two players play the following impartial game. The state is a collection of piles given by the list `piles` of pile sizes. On a turn, the current player picks one pile of size s and splits it into two NON-EMPTY piles of UNEQUAL sizes a and b (a + b = s, a != b). A pile that cannot be split this way (size 1, or size 2 since the only split 1+1 is equal) is dead. A player who cannot make any move loses. Both players play optimally.

Implement `entry(piles)` returning True if the FIRST player to move wins, and False otherwise.

For a non-negative integer x, define its alternating digit sum as d0 - d1 + d2 - d3 + ..., where d0 is the LEAST significant digit, d1 the next, and so on (the sign alternates, starting with + on the units digit). You are given integers lo, hi (0 <= lo <= hi) and a positive integer M. Count how many integers x with lo <= x <= hi have an alternating digit sum that is divisible by M (a value v is divisible by M when v mod M == 0, using Python's modulo for negatives).

Implement `entry(lo, hi, M)` returning that count as an int.

Count the number of distinct necklaces of length n using k colors, where two necklaces are considered the same if one can be obtained from the other by a ROTATION (cyclic shift). Reflections are NOT considered equivalent. Implement `entry(n, k)` returning the count modulo 1000000007 as an int. By convention, for n == 0 the answer is 1.
bruteforce:
def entry(n, k):
    MOD=10**9+7
    if n==0: return 1
    import itertools
    seen=set()
    for s in itertools.product(range(k), repeat=n):
        rots=[tuple(s[i:]+s[:i]) for i in range(n)]
        canon=min(rots)
        seen.add(canon)
    return len(seen)%MOD

prompt:
You are given a tree with n nodes labeled 0..n-1 (n >= 2), an edge list `edges` (n-1 undirected edges), and a list `vals` where `vals[i]` is the integer value of node i. Remove exactly ONE edge, which splits the tree into two connected components. The cost of that removal is the absolute difference between the sum of node values in the two components.

Implement `entry(n, edges, vals)` returning the minimum possible cost over all choices of edge to remove, as an int.

You are given a list `points` of integers (which may be unsorted and may contain duplicates) and a positive integer `g`. Choose a maximum-size subset of the points such that any two chosen points differ by at least `g` in absolute value. Implement `entry(points, g)` returning the maximum number of points that can be chosen, as an int. For an empty list the answer is 0.

You are given a `grid` of 0s and 1s with R rows and C columns (R,C >= 1). You move on a token that starts by ENTERING cell (0,0) and wants to reach cell (R-1,C-1). From any cell you may step to an orthogonally adjacent cell (up/down/left/right). You carry a parity bit that starts at 0 BEFORE entering (0,0); every time you ENTER a cell whose value is 1, the parity bit flips (XOR with 1). The movement rule: you may ENTER a cell with value 1 only if your CURRENT parity (before the flip) is 0; you may always enter a cell with value 0. This rule applies to entering (0,0) as well (with starting parity 0). Cells may be revisited; the relevant state is (row, col, parity).

Implement `entry(grid)` returning True if (R-1,C-1) is reachable under these rules, otherwise False.
bruteforce:
def entry(grid):
    R=len(grid); C=len(grid[0])
    def can_enter(v,parity):
        if v==1 and parity==1: return False
        return True
    sv=grid[0][0]
    if not can_enter(sv,0): return False
    seen=set()
    found=[False]
    import sys
    sys.setrecursionlimit(100000)
    def dfs(r,c,p):
        if found[0]: return
        if (r,c,p) in seen: return
        seen.add((r,c,p))
        if r==R-1 and c==C-1:
            found[0]=True; return
        for dr,dc in ((1,0),(-1,0),(0,1),(0,-1)):
            nr,nc=r+dr,c+dc
            if 0<=nr<R and 0<=nc<C:
                v=grid[nr][nc]
                if can_enter(v,p):
                    dfs(nr,nc,p^v)
    dfs(0,0,0^sv)
    return found[0]

prompt: Count the number of ordered compositions of the integer n (n >= 0) into parts, where each part is an integer between 1 and `kmax` inclusive, AND no two ADJACENT parts in the composition are equal. The order of parts matters (a composition is a sequence of parts summing to n). Implement `entry(n, kmax)` returning the count modulo 1000000007 as an int. By convention, n == 0 has exactly 1 composition (the empty one).

You are given a tree with n nodes labeled 0..n-1, described by an edge list `edges` (n-1 undirected edges, or empty when n==1). A matching is a set of edges no two of which share a common vertex; the empty set counts as a matching. Implement `entry(n, edges)` returning the total number of matchings of the tree, modulo 1000000007, as an int.
reference:
def entry(n, edges):
    MOD=10**9+7
    g=[[] for _ in range(n)]
    for a,b in edges:
        g[a].append(b); g[b].append(a)
    parent=[-1]*n; order=[]; visited=[False]*n
    stack=[0]; visited[0]=True
    while stack:
        u=stack.pop(); order.append(u)
        for v in g[u]:
            if not visited[v]:
                visited[v]=True; parent[v]=u; stack.append(v)
    dp0=[1]*n
    dp1=[0]*n
    for u in reversed(order):
        children=[v for v in g[u] if v!=parent[u]]
        prod_free=1
        for c in children:
            prod_free=prod_free*((dp0[c]+dp1[c])%MOD)%MOD
        dp0[u]=prod_free
        s=0
        for c in children:
            free_c=dp0[c]
            others=prod_free * pow((dp0[c]+dp1[c])%MOD, MOD-2, MOD) % MOD
            s=(s + free_c*others)%MOD
        dp1[u]=s
    return (dp0[0]+dp1[0])%MOD

prompt: Count the binary strings of length n that satisfy BOTH conditions: (1) no three consecutive characters are equal (no run of identical characters has length >= 3), and (2) the string contains exactly as many '0's as '1's. Implement `entry(n)` returning the count modulo 1000000007 as an int. If n is odd the answer is 0; for n == 0 the answer is 1.

You are given a tree with n nodes labeled 0..n-1, an edge list `edges` (n-1 undirected edges, or empty when n==1), and an integer k (number of colors). Color each node with one of k colors. A coloring is GOOD if there is no node b that has two or more neighbors sharing b's own color. (Equivalently: for every node b, the number of neighbors of b whose color equals color[b] is at most 1; equivalently no length-2 path a-b-c exists with color[a]==color[b]==color[c].)

Implement `entry(n, edges, k)` returning the number of good colorings modulo 1000000007 as an int.

You tile a 2-by-`n` board completely with 1x2 dominoes. Each domino is placed either vertically (covering both cells of one column) or horizontally (covering two cells in the same row across two adjacent columns). Count the number of complete tilings in which the number of VERTICAL dominoes used is EVEN (zero counts as even). Implement `entry(n)` returning that count modulo 1000000007. n is an integer with 0 <= n <= 30. For n=0 there is exactly one tiling (the empty one) with 0 vertical dominoes, so the answer is 1.

There are `n` lamps in a row indexed 0..n-1, all initially OFF. A single move chooses an index i (0 <= i <= n-1) and toggles lamp i and also lamp i+2 if i+2 <= n-1 (no wraparound; if i+2 is out of range only lamp i toggles). Given a target string `target` of '0'/'1' of length n (n = len(target)), return the MINIMUM number of moves to turn the all-OFF configuration into `target`, or -1 if it is impossible. Implement `entry(target)`.

On an `r` x `c` grid you start at cell (0,0) and want to reach (r-1, c-1), moving only Right (j+1) or Down (i+1) one step at a time. You may NOT enter any cell (i,j) for which (i + j) % 3 == 0, with the SOLE exception of the start cell (0,0) and the destination cell (r-1,c-1), which are always allowed even if their coordinate sum is divisible by 3. Implement `entry(r, c)` returning the number of valid paths (an int). If start or end is blocked-by-rule it is still allowed, but if no path exists return 0.

You are given a permutation `p` of 1..n (a list) and an integer `k`. You process `p` left to right using ONE stack that can hold at most `k` items simultaneously. At each step you may either push the next input element onto the stack (only if the stack currently holds fewer than k items) or pop the top of the stack to the output. You must produce the output sequence 1, 2, 3, ..., n in that exact increasing order. Implement `entry(p, k)` returning True if this is achievable and False otherwise. n = len(p); 1 <= k.
reference:
def entry(p, k):
    n = len(p)
    stack = []
    want = 1
    i = 0
    while want <= n:
        if stack and stack[-1] == want:
            stack.pop()
            want += 1
            continue
        if i < n and len(stack) < k:
            stack.append(p[i])
            i += 1
            continue
        return False
    return True

prompt: Given a list `a` of non-negative integers and an integer `x`, count the number of subsequences (chosen by index subset, the empty subsequence included) whose XOR of all chosen elements equals `x` AND whose number of chosen elements is a multiple of 3 (0 counts as a multiple of 3, and the empty subsequence has XOR 0). Two subsequences are different if their chosen index sets differ. Implement `entry(a, x)` returning the count modulo 1000000007.
reference:
def entry(a, x):
    MOD = 1000000007
    from collections import defaultdict
    dp = defaultdict(int)
    dp[(0, 0)] = 1
    for v in a:
        ndp = defaultdict(int)
        for (xv, lm), cnt in dp.items():
            ndp[(xv, lm)] = (ndp[(xv, lm)] + cnt) % MOD
            nx = xv ^ v
            nl = (lm + 1) % 3
            ndp[(nx, nl)] = (ndp[(nx, nl)] + cnt) % MOD
        dp = ndp
    return dp.get((x, 0), 0) % MOD

prompt: A single pile starts with `n` tokens (n >= 0). Two players alternate moves; the first player moves first. On a move a player removes from the pile either a positive perfect-square number of tokens (1, 4, 9, 16, ...) or exactly 2 tokens (2 is allowed even though it is not a perfect square). A move is only legal if the pile has at least that many tokens. A player who cannot move (pile empty) loses. Both play optimally. Implement `entry(n)` returning the string "First" if the first player wins, otherwise "Second".

Consider all binary strings of length `n` that contain exactly `k` ones (so `n - k` zeros). Define two strings equivalent if one can be obtained from the other by any sequence of these two operations: (a) cyclic rotation by one position (repeatable), and (b) flipping every bit (0<->1) of the whole string. Implement `entry(n, k)` returning the number of distinct equivalence classes among those length-n strings with exactly k ones (an int). Note operation (b) changes the count of ones from k to n-k, so a class may contain strings with k ones and strings with n-k ones; count each class once, considering it 'present' if it contains at least one string with exactly k ones. 0 <= k <= n, n >= 0.

You are given a string `s` consisting of characters '(' , ')' and '?'. Replace each '?' independently by either '(' or ')'. Count the number of resulting strings that satisfy ALL of these conditions: (1) the string is a balanced bracket sequence (every prefix has at least as many '(' as ')', and the totals are equal); (2) no two ADJACENT characters of the final string are identical (i.e. you never have "((" or "))"). Implement `entry(s)` returning the count modulo 1000000007. The empty string counts as balanced with the adjacency condition vacuously true.

Given an `r` x `c` grid `g` of non-negative integers, you travel from cell (0,0) to cell (r-1,c-1). Normally you move one step Right or Down and collect the value of every cell you stand on. Additionally, AT MOST ONCE during the trip you may use a teleport: from your current cell (i,j) you instantly jump to any cell (i2,j2) with i2 >= i and j2 >= j and (i2,j2) != (i,j); cells skipped by the teleport are NOT collected, and the destination cell IS collected (once). After teleporting you continue normal R/D movement. Each cell's value is counted at most once even if you could otherwise revisit. Implement `entry(g)` returning the maximum total collected value (an int). The grid has r = len(g) >= 1 rows and c = len(g[0]) >= 1 columns.

Given a list `a` of positive integers, find the length of the longest subsequence (strictly increasing indices) a[i1], a[i2], ..., a[im] such that for every consecutive pair, a[i_{t+1}] is divisible by a[i_t] AND the quotient a[i_{t+1}] / a[i_t] is a prime number (a single prime, e.g. quotient 6 = 2*3 is NOT allowed, but quotient 5 is). A subsequence of length 1 is always valid. Implement `entry(a)` returning the maximum length (an int). If `a` is empty return 0.

You are given a permutation `p` of 0..n-1 arranged on a CIRCLE of n positions (n = len(p)). In one move you may swap the elements in two positions that are adjacent on the circle; positions i and i+1 are adjacent for 0 <= i < n-1, and additionally position n-1 is adjacent to position 0 (wraparound). Implement `entry(p)` returning the MINIMUM number of such adjacent swaps needed to reach the identity permutation [0,1,...,n-1]. For n <= 1 the answer is 0.

You are given non-negative integers `s`, `t` and a base `b` (2 <= b <= 16). In one operation you replace the current value v by v + S(v), where S(v) is the sum of the digits of v WHEN WRITTEN IN BASE b. Starting from `s`, you repeatedly apply the operation. Implement `entry(s, t, b)` returning the minimum number of operations to make the value exactly equal to `t`, or -1 if `t` can never be reached (the value only increases for v>0, so if it ever exceeds t without hitting it, it's unreachable). If s == t the answer is 0.

You are given a rooted tree on n nodes (node 0 is the root). `parent` is a list of length n where parent[0] = -1 and parent[i] (for i>=1) is the parent of node i. `coins` is a list of length n of 0/1 values. A move selects any node u and FLIPS (0<->1) the coin on u and the coins on all of u's DIRECT children (not deeper descendants). Find the minimum number of moves to make every coin 0, or -1 if impossible. Implement `entry(parent, coins)`. n = len(coins) >= 1. Each node may be selected any number of times (only parity of selections matters).

A strip has `n` cells indexed 0..n-1, all initially color 0. You are given a list `ops` of operations; each op is a triple [l, r, color] (0 <= l <= r <= n-1, color is a positive integer) that, when applied, paints every cell in the inclusive range l..r with `color`, overwriting whatever was there. You process a chosen SUBSET of the ops in their ORIGINAL relative order (you may skip any ops, but cannot reorder). Over all 2^k choices of subset (k = len(ops)), count the number of DISTINCT final colorings of the whole strip. Implement `entry(n, ops)` returning that count (an int). The all-zero coloring (skip everything) counts.

Count the number of sequences (a_1, a_2, ..., a_n) where each a_i is an integer in [1, m], no two ADJACENT elements are equal (a_i != a_{i+1} for all valid i), AND the total sum a_1 + ... + a_n is divisible by 3. Implement `entry(n, m)` returning the count modulo 1000000007. For n = 0 there is exactly one sequence (the empty one) whose sum 0 is divisible by 3, so return 1. 0 <= n, 1 <= m.

Count the balanced bracket sequences using exactly `n` '(' and `n` ')' characters (length 2n, every prefix has #'(' >= #')' and totals equal) whose MAXIMUM nesting depth equals EXACTLY `d`. The nesting depth at a position is the number of currently-open brackets; the maximum depth of the sequence is the largest such value over all prefixes. Implement `entry(n, d)` returning the count (an int). For n = 0 the only sequence is empty with max depth 0, so entry(0,0)=1 and entry(0,d)=0 for d>0. 0 <= d.

A knight starts at cell (0,0) on an `r` x `c` chessboard (rows 0..r-1, columns 0..c-1). It moves like a standard chess knight (the eight (+-1,+-2)/(+-2,+-1) offsets), never leaving the board. Implement `entry(r, c, k)` returning the number of DISTINCT cells (including the starting cell) that the knight can be on after AT MOST `k` moves (i.e. reachable using 0, 1, 2, ..., or k moves). r >= 1, c >= 1, k >= 0.

You are given a rectangular grid of lights as a list of rows, each row a list of 0/1 ints. The grid has R rows (R<=12) and C columns (C<=4); the grid may also be empty (no rows). You process the grid one column at a time, independently. For a single column you may perform AT MOST ONE operation: either do nothing, OR choose a contiguous vertical segment of rows [i..j] (0<=i<=j<R) and TOGGLE (flip 0<->1) every cell of that column inside the segment. A column is 'cleared' if after your (optional) operation all of its cells are 0. For each column count how many distinct valid choices (the 'do nothing' option, plus every distinct contiguous segment) result in that column being fully cleared; the total number of ways for the whole grid is the PRODUCT over all columns of these per-column counts. Implement `entry(grid)` returning that product modulo 1_000_000_007. For an empty grid return 1.

There is a single pile of n stones (1<=n<=40). Two players alternate moves; the first player moves first. A move applies to the current single pile of size s: you must split it into two piles of UNEQUAL positive sizes a and b (a != b, a+b = s), and then immediately REMOVE the smaller of the two new piles entirely, leaving only the larger pile (of size max(a,b)) in play. A player who cannot move LOSES; you cannot move when the current pile size s satisfies s<=1, or when s has no unequal split (i.e. s==2, whose only split 1+1 is equal). Both players play optimally. Implement `entry(n)` returning True if the first player wins, otherwise False.

Given two integers L and R with 0 <= L <= R <= 1_000_000, count the integers x in the inclusive range [L, R] that satisfy BOTH of these conditions: (1) the decimal digit sum of x is even (the digit sum of 0 is 0, which is even), and (2) x is divisible by (digit_sum(x) + 1). Implement `entry(L, R)` returning that count as a plain int.

You are given an undirected graph with n vertices labeled 0..n-1 (1<=n<=8) and a list `edges` of [u, v] pairs, plus an integer k (number of available colors, 1<=k<=4) whose colors are labeled 0..k-1. Count the proper colorings of the graph (every edge's two endpoints get different colors) subject to the EXTRA constraint that color 0 is used on an EVEN number of vertices (using it 0 times counts as even). Implement `entry(n, edges, k)` returning that count modulo 1_000_000_007.

A frog starts on step 0 and wants to reach step n exactly (0 <= n <= 30). Each jump moves the frog forward by either +1 or +2 steps. The frog must land exactly on step n (it may not overshoot). There is one restriction: the frog may NOT perform a +2 jump immediately followed by another +2 jump (no two consecutive +2 jumps anywhere in the sequence; +1 jumps have no restriction). Count the number of distinct ordered jump sequences that take the frog from step 0 to step n. The empty sequence (for n==0) counts as 1. Implement `entry(n)` returning that count modulo 1_000_000_007.

There are n workers and n tasks, where the tasks are exactly the integer indices 0..n-1 (0<=n<=7). You are given a list `allowed` of length n; `allowed[i]` is the list of task indices (each in 0..n-1) that worker i is permitted to do. You must assign every worker a DISTINCT task (a perfect matching of workers to tasks, each worker using a task from its own allowed list). There is an additional constraint: for every pair of consecutive workers i and i+1, the task indices assigned to them must have DIFFERENT parity (one even, one odd). Count the number of valid full assignments. Implement `entry(allowed)` returning that count as a plain int. For n==0 return 1 (the empty assignment).

You are given a grid as a list of equal-length strings (up to 4 rows by 4 columns; the grid may be empty). Each character is '.' (open), '#' (wall), 'S' (the unique start cell, open), or 'E' (the unique exit cell, open). From the current cell you may move one step in any of the 4 orthogonal directions onto a cell that is inside the grid and not a wall. You start at 'S' having made 0 moves. You may FINISH on 'E' only if you arrive there after an EVEN total number of moves (0 is even, so if S itself were E that counts). Implement `entry(grid)` returning the MINIMUM number of moves to be standing on 'E' with an even move count, or -1 if impossible (including when there is no 'S' or the grid is empty).

You are given a list `intervals`, each element being [start, end, color] with integer start < end and color in {0,1} (list length n <= 10). Choose a SUBSET of these intervals that are pairwise non-overlapping, where two intervals do not overlap if one ends no later than the other begins (touching at an endpoint is allowed, i.e. a.end <= b.start). When the chosen intervals are sorted by start time, their colors must STRICTLY ALTERNATE (the sequence reads 0,1,0,1,... or 1,0,1,0,...). Maximize the COUNT of chosen intervals. Implement `entry(intervals)` returning the maximum achievable count as a plain int (0 if intervals is empty or no interval can be chosen).

Given an odd prime p (3 <= p <= 97) and a positive integer k (1 <= k <= 20), count the residues x in the set {1, 2, ..., p-1} such that x^k is congruent to 1 modulo p. Implement `entry(p, k)` returning that count as a plain int. (The answer counts solutions of the congruence directly; it equals gcd(k, p-1), but you must produce the count.)

You are given a tree with n nodes labeled 0..n-1 (1 <= n <= 8): `edges` is a list of n-1 pairs [u, v], and `w` is a list of n integer node weights (weights may be negative). Define a leaf as a node whose degree is <= 1; when n==1 the single node is a leaf. Choose a NON-EMPTY independent set of nodes (no two chosen nodes are adjacent) that MUST include at least one leaf, maximizing the sum of chosen node weights. Since selecting a single leaf alone is always allowed, a valid choice always exists. Implement `entry(n, edges, w)` returning the maximum achievable total weight as a plain int.

Count the strings of length L (0 <= L <= 12) over the two-letter alphabet {'a','b'} that satisfy BOTH conditions: (1) the string does NOT contain `pattern` as a contiguous substring, and (2) the string contains an EVEN number of 'a' characters (0 occurrences is even). `pattern` is a non-empty string over {'a','b'} with length at most 4. (If pattern were empty, no string could avoid it, so the answer would be 0; you may assume pattern is non-empty.) Implement `entry(L, pattern)` returning the count modulo 1_000_000_007.

There is a single pile of n stones (0 <= n <= 60). Two players alternate moves; the first player moves first. A move removes a positive perfect-square number of stones (1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, ...) that does not exceed the current pile size. This is a MISERE game: the player who removes the LAST stone (leaving the pile empty) LOSES. Equivalently, define position 0 (a player facing an empty pile, unable to move) as a WIN for the player to move, because the opponent must have taken the last stone and thus lost. Both players play optimally. Implement `entry(n)` returning True if the first player wins, otherwise False.

You are given a directed graph on n nodes labeled 0..n-1 (0 <= n <= 7) and a list `edges` of directed pairs [u, v] meaning there is an edge u -> v. Count the Hamiltonian paths (orderings that visit every node exactly once, following directed edges between consecutive visited nodes) whose FIRST node has an EVEN index and whose LAST node has an ODD index. Implement `entry(n, edges)` returning that count as a plain int. For n==0 return 0; for n==1 the single node cannot both start even and end odd, so the answer is 0.

A standard six-sided die (opposite faces sum to 7) sits on a grid given as a list of equal-length strings of '.' (open) and '#' (blocked), up to 3 rows by 3 columns; the grid may be empty. The die starts on the top-left cell (row 0, col 0), which is guaranteed open, in the fixed orientation top=1, north=2, east=3. The die can roll one cell North, South, East, or West onto an adjacent open cell that lies inside the grid (it may not roll off the grid or onto a '#'). Rolling re-orients the die in the natural way (the face that was on the leading side becomes the bottom, etc.). A state is the pair (cell, orientation), where orientation is captured by the (top, north, east) face triple. Count the number of DISTINCT reachable states, including the starting state. Implement `entry(grid)` returning that count as a plain int (0 for an empty grid). Rolling conventions: from (top, north, east) with bottom=7-top, south=7-north, west=7-east — North gives (south, top, east); South gives (north, bottom, east); East gives (west, north, top); West gives (east, north, bottom).

Given a target integer n (0 <= n <= 30) and a list `parts` of distinct positive integers, count the COMPOSITIONS of n: ordered sequences of values drawn from `parts` (with repetition allowed across the sequence) whose sum is exactly n, subject to the constraint that no two ADJACENT parts in the sequence are equal. The empty composition (which sums to 0) counts as 1 valid way when n==0. Implement `entry(n, parts)` returning the count modulo 1_000_000_007. (Duplicate values in `parts` should be treated as a single value.)

You are given a grid of non-negative integers as a list of equal-length rows (up to 4 rows by 4 columns; assume at least one cell) and an integer `target`. Count the monotone lattice paths from the top-left cell (0,0) to the bottom-right cell (R-1, C-1) that move only RIGHT or DOWN one cell at a time, such that the bitwise XOR of ALL visited cell values (including both the start and end cells) equals `target`. Implement `entry(grid, target)` returning that count modulo 1_000_000_007.

Two piles of stones have sizes a and b (0 <= a, b <= 12). Two players alternate moves; the first player moves first. On a turn the player chooses ONE of the two piles and removes between 1 and k stones from it (1 <= k <= 4), where the amount removed may not exceed that pile's current size. This is normal play: a player who cannot move (both piles are already empty) LOSES. Both players play optimally. Implement `entry(a, b, k)` returning True if the first player wins, otherwise False.
